Q1: Honestly, I didn't really have much of a routine. I'd study when I felt like it, which wasn't super often. Most weeks I'd put in maybe 17 hours, but it was scattered—some days nothing, then a random burst when I remembered I had something due. I learn best by doing things with my hands, so sitting and reading online materials felt tedious and I'd drift off mentally.
Q2: My pattern was all over the place, mostly driven by deadlines creeping up on me. I'd go days without touching course stuff, then suddenly realize something was due and cram. Regular study times sound nice in theory, but I just couldn't make myself stick to a schedule when the material didn't grab me. Work and life stuff also meant my availability shifted constantly.
Q3: Usually it was either a deadline reminder email or that nagging guilt that I hadn't checked in for a while. Sometimes I'd log in just to see if I'd missed anything important, scroll around for a bit, then log out without doing much. The gamified task thing they introduced actually got me to log in a few extra times because it felt less boring than regular assignments.
Q4: Not really, no. It never became automatic for me. I'd have brief stretches where I'd check in more regularly, especially when the gamified elements made it feel less like a chore. But then I'd lose interest again and go back to only logging in when absolutely necessary. The routine just never stuck.
Q5: The video content was probably the most useful because I could watch it while doing other things or rewind parts I didn't catch. I also liked when there were interactive elements or hands-on activities because that's how my brain actually absorbs information. Discussion posts were okay when people actually said interesting things, but that was hit or miss.
Q6: I basically ignored most of the reading materials and supplementary resources. They felt dry and I knew I could probably get by without them. I'd skim assignment instructions at the last minute rather than reading them thoroughly when they were posted. If something looked optional and boring, it wasn't happening.
Q7: If I could see an immediate point to it or if it was actually engaging, I'd do it. The gamified tasks felt worth my time because there was something satisfying about completing them, like a small win. But if something felt like busywork or just checking boxes, I couldn't convince myself to care enough to put real effort in.
Q8: Yeah, optional stuff basically didn't exist for me. If it wasn't going to affect my grade, why bother? Required activities I'd do, but usually at the last possible moment. There's something about knowing you have to do something that at least creates enough pressure to make it happen, even if I'm not enthusiastic about it.
Q9: Definitely. I'd download lecture slides and PDFs, then study from those on my phone during my commute or at work during slow periods. I also used YouTube videos from other people explaining the same concepts, sometimes more clearly than our course materials. My actual learning happened a lot outside the LMS.
Q10: There were plenty of times I'd be going over downloaded notes or working through problems on paper, and none of that showed up anywhere. I'd also talk through concepts with a friend who was in a similar program, which helped me understand things way better than staring at a screen. The LMS only saw me when I was clicking around, not when I was actually thinking.
Q11: All the time. I'd download a video or PDF once, then never go back to the LMS for that material again. Why would I keep logging in when I already had what I needed saved locally? It was more convenient to have everything on my devices where I could access it whenever, even without internet.
Q12: There were definitely weeks where I just couldn't bring myself to care. Nothing specifically bad was happening—I just felt bored and disconnected from the material. When you're not passionate about something, every login feels like a chore. The course content didn't excite me, so engagement felt forced.
Q13: Work was a big factor. I have limited resources so I need to work, and sometimes after a long shift, the last thing I wanted was to stare at more screens. When I was tired, I'd log in and just kind of zone out, not really absorbing anything. Competing responsibilities definitely won over coursework most of the time.
Q14: Oh yeah, plenty of times. I'd have the LMS open, maybe a video playing, but I'd be mentally somewhere else entirely. Just going through the motions without anything sticking. It's like my body was present but my brain had checked out. Those sessions were basically useless even though they probably looked productive in the data.
Q15: I postponed assignments constantly, even knowing they mattered. It's like I couldn't make myself start until the pressure was unbearable. Part of it was boredom, part of it was just not seeing the immediate relevance. I'd tell myself I'd do it tomorrow, and tomorrow would become the night before it was due.
Q16: Deadlines were basically the only thing that got me to actually submit work. Without them, I probably would have done even less. I'd see a deadline approaching and finally kick into gear, cramming everything into a short window. It's not a great system but it's the only thing that worked for me.
Q17: Feedback didn't really change my behavior much. By the time I got it, I'd already moved on mentally to the next thing or back to not thinking about the course. If the feedback was positive, cool. If it was critical, I'd acknowledge it but not necessarily change anything. It just didn't feel urgent enough to act on.
Q18: The structure was pretty standard, which meant it was easy to drift through without really engaging. Having set modules and deadlines gave some framework, but it wasn't compelling enough to make me want to participate beyond the minimum. The gamified task was a nice change that actually got my attention for a bit.
Q19: The gamified elements definitely made engagement easier—they broke up the monotony. But the heavy reliance on reading and passive video watching made it harder for someone like me who needs to actually do things to learn. More hands-on activities would have helped me stay interested.
Q20: I think some people might be like me—logging in, clicking around, maybe watching videos but not actually doing the work. You can look busy without being productive. Or maybe they're struggling to understand what's expected and spinning their wheels. High activity doesn't mean high output.
Q21: That's basically me. I didn't log in much, but I studied in my own way offline and did well enough on exams. I understand concepts better through my own methods than through the LMS interface. Low activity just means I wasn't visible online, not that I wasn't learning.
Q22: Logs can show when and how often you clicked on things, but they can't show if you actually understood or cared about any of it. They miss all the offline studying, the thinking, the conversations with peers. They're measuring presence, not learning. It's a pretty shallow picture.
Q23: Being engaged means actually caring about what you're learning and wanting to understand it, not just going through motions to get a grade. It's when the material connects to something real for you and you're mentally present. For me, that happened occasionally but not consistently in this course.
Q24: The LMS wouldn't show that I actually did learn the material well enough to score decently on exams, despite my low visible engagement. It wouldn't capture my offline studying, my conversations with friends about the content, or the fact that I was bored rather than struggling. Boredom and disengagement look the same as incompetence in the data, but they're very different.
